sections.title: Where I Begin, Where I Flow
sections.content_summary: The chapter opens with a poem and introduces the Godavari river narrating its own journey. Godavari begins as a stream at Trimbakeshwar in the Western Ghats (Maharashtra), gathers tributaries along its 1,465-kilometre course, and joins the Bay of Bengal, making it the second longest river in India after the Ganga. The river is also known as Dakshina Ganga and is regarded as sacred, with the Godavari Pushkaram festival drawing pilgrims to its banks at Nashik.

tasks.task_text: Look at the image given below and answer the following. 1. Name the states that Godavari flows through. 2. Name a dam and a wildlife sanctuary along the length of Godavari. 3. Which other small rivers join Godavari in its journey?

sections.title: The Life I Hold
sections.content_summary: This section explains that Godavari is a perennial river (flowing year-round), as distinct from seasonal rivers that only flow in the rainy season. The river passes through rich forests home to tigers, barking deer, Indian golden gecko, and red sandalwood trees. At its mouth, Godavari spreads into a delta where freshwater meets the sea at the Coringa Mangrove Forests.

sections.title: The Many Ways I Support Lives
sections.content_summary: Since ancient times, human settlements have been built near rivers for reliable access to water. Godavari supplies water for drinking, cooking, washing, irrigation, and industrial use, reaching homes and fields through pipes and canals. People engage in agriculture (rice cultivation visible in the textbook illustration), fishing, and ecotourism along its banks, making the river central to the livelihoods of many towns and villages.

tasks.task_text: Since ancient times, people have always built their homes near rivers or water bodies. Can you guess why?
List three ways in which people are dependent on the river Godavari.
In which occupations are people engaged near the river Godavari?
Which crops do you see growing near the Godavari?
Which crops grow in your region? Where does the water for irrigation come from?
Make a poster in your classroom showing all the ways rivers help people. Include drawings of homes, farms, boats, fishermen and more.

sections.title: Dams—Storing My Water, Changing My Flow
sections.content_summary: India has more than 900 dams that store river water for use in dry seasons—for drinking, irrigation, and electricity generation. A dam creates a reservoir by blocking the river's flow, but this floods land that was previously home to wildlife and people, forcing displacement. The section encourages students to consider the trade-offs of dam construction—the benefits of stored water against the costs to people and ecosystems displaced.

tasks.task_text: What problems can arise after a dam is built? How can they be solved?

sections.title: What Pollutes Me and Why it Matters
sections.content_summary: Waste, plastic, and untreated sewage from expanding settlements and factories pollute river water, making it unsafe for both people and wildlife. The section introduces the concept of solubility—some pollutants dissolve invisibly in water while others (like oil) do not, both posing harm. Fertiliser runoff causes algal overgrowth (the 'green blanket' effect) that chokes aquatic life and renders water undrinkable. The Namami Gange programme is cited as an example of organised river-cleaning effort.

tasks.task_text: Where do the plastic wrappers and bags that we litter end up?
What kinds of soaps, shampoos or floor cleaners (natural or readymade) are used in your home? Where does the unclean water mixed with these cleaners go?
Ask your parents or elders, about the condition of water bodies near your home when they were your age. What is the status of those water bodies today?

Make a list of things that we throw into the waste bin at home or school. If these things were thrown into a river or a pond, how would each of these items harm the animals, plants and people who live nearby, and use this water?
Take two clear glasses of water. Add a spoonful of sugar in one and a spoonful of cooking oil in the other. Stir both well and observe. What do you observe? Which one appears clear?
Look at the two images given below. Which river looks healthy and why? How do so many plants grow in water? What do you think happens when too many plants grow in a river?
Can you list three things you can do to protect the water bodies near you?

sections.title: When a River Floods
sections.content_summary: When rainfall is excessive, rivers overflow their banks, flooding homes, fields, and roads, and forcing both people and animals to evacuate. Deforestation worsens flooding because tree cover slows rainwater runoff and helps soil absorb water. The chapter provides a Flood Safety Tips infographic covering actions to take before, during, and after a flood, and asks students to think about civic disaster preparedness.

tasks.task_text: Match the Columns. Flood problem: (i) Dirty water (ii) Broken roads (iii) Lost crops (iv) No electricity (v) Spread of diseases (vi) Damaged homes (vii) Schools shut — What it affects: (a) Shelter for people (b) Safe drinking (c) Learning (d) Travel and transport (e) Lights, fans and phones (f) People's health (g) Food supply

Read the flood safety tips given in Activity 6 and organise a role-play where you are the Sarpanch or Municipal Corporator of your village or city. Discuss what actions you will take to— prevent flooding / prevent damage caused by flooding / keep elderly people and people with disability safe / keep animals safe

sections.title: When a River Runs Dry!
sections.content_summary: Drought and water scarcity cause hardship parallel to flooding—families and animals may be forced to migrate when water runs out. The section uses the example of Chennai (2019 water crisis) and Bengaluru (2022 floods) to show how both extremes threaten lives. Students are encouraged to reflect on personal water use and adopt conservation habits; the National Water Mission and water ATMs are cited as systemic conservation efforts.

tasks.task_text: What would you do if you had no water in your home for an entire day?
What if there was no water for a week? How would you bath, drink, cook or clean? Where would you go to find water?

sections.title: Let us reflect
sections.content_summary: The closing reflection section asks students to connect the chapter to their own region and lived experience—finding local rivers, hearing family stories about rivers, imagining civic water management, and designing simple water-filter prototypes. It consolidates the chapter's dual emphasis on understanding rivers as natural systems and as resources that require active human stewardship.

tasks.task_text: Find out which river(s) flow through your state. (a) What are they used for? (b) Are there any dams on them?
Ask your grandparents or parents to share any story or festival related to rivers in your region and present it in the class.
Ask an elder in your family: (a) What was the condition of rivers when you were a child? (b) What has changed since then?
Why do you think people built houses and cities near rivers in the past? Are rivers still important today in the same way?
If you could create one new rule to protect rivers in your city or village, what would it be?
Think about a time when you wasted water. What could you have done differently?
Can you design a simple water filter using everyday materials? What would you include and why?

Imagine you are planning a new city near a river. What steps would you take to: (a) keep the river clean. (b) prevent floods during heavy rains. (c) ensure there is always enough water for everyone, even during summers.

dual_strand.natural: River systems — source, tributaries, delta, perennial vs seasonal flow, flooding, and drought
dual_strand.human_cultural: River dependency for livelihood, sacred traditions (Godavari Pushkaram), dam trade-offs, pollution and conservation, civic disaster preparedness
